content.assessment: This proposal does not touch selection, admissions, or governance mechanisms at all; it is a resourcing intervention layered on top of an unchanged institutional structure. The lens findings on selection share, tracking age, and segregation remain fully applicable regardless of investment levels, since quality investment does not alter who is eligible to select or when selection occurs.
opportunities: Could reduce disparities in outcomes documented for disadvantaged students (12.1% reaching a good skill level) without requiring governance renegotiation
strengths: Does not require altering centralized governance arrangements, so implementation may proceed without conflict over admissions law or maintainer autonomy
threats: Because selection capacity remains at 85% of schools, quality investment in comprehensive schools may not reduce demand for selective tracks if track access continues to function as an institutionally protected sorting mechanism independent of quality perception
weaknesses: Leaves unchanged the core selection and tracking mechanisms (early ages, high selective-school share) that structurally produce sorting independent of school quality
Does not address the differentiated legal status of church/private schools implicated in segregation precedent
